questions.title: Where does precedence resolution live?
questions.context: Phase 1 ended with admission logic split between packages/intent (ambiguity) and packages/policy (capability + repo-scope + archetype). GOV-01 adds a 4-tier precedence stack (confirmed intent ⟶ policy ⟶ repo instructions ⟶ operator settings). Today nothing in the codebase merges these tiers — admission only checks intent-vs-policy. STRUCTURE.md treats packages/policy as 'authority and admission'.

options.label: New packages/governance workspace
options.description: Create a sibling to packages/policy that owns precedence merge + conflict evidence. Policy keeps doing pure capability checks; governance composes. Cleanest layering, one new package to wire.

options.label: Extend packages/policy
options.description: Add a precedence-resolution.ts module inside policy. No new package; reuses existing public-split-exports contract. Risk: policy becomes a god-package — already has ambiguity, capability, repo-scope, archetype.

options.label: New packages/authority workspace
options.description: Same as (a) but named to match the GOV requirement language ('authority boundary'). Aligns with the AGENTS.md vocabulary already used elsewhere.

questions.title: Conflict resolution algorithm
questions.context: GOV-01 says 'conflicts produce explicit deny/allow with evidence'. Today there is no merge function — the four sources don't even all exist (operator settings has no schema yet). Need to decide the resolution shape now to lock evidence format.

options.label: Ordered first-match (highest tier wins)
options.description: Confirmed intent > policy > repo instructions > operator settings. First tier to deny blocks; first tier to allow allows; lower tiers can only narrow, never widen. Simple, explainable. Forces every tier to be denial-only or fully-defined.

options.label: Intersection / strictest-wins
options.description: Every tier is a constraint set; final envelope = intersection of all tiers. Cannot widen by definition. Matches capability-envelope mental model. Harder to explain conflicts since 'who denied' may not be unique.

options.label: Per-rule precedence with scored evidence
options.description: Each rule (e.g. 'network egress to host X') resolves independently with a per-rule precedence rule. Most flexible. Evidence is per-rule. Requires a rule registry — new infrastructure.

questions.title: Repo instructions — source file(s)
questions.context: REQUIREMENTS.md mentions 'repo instructions' as a precedence tier but never specifies the file. AGENTS.md exists at repo root. Phase 3 will own actual repo I/O; Phase 2 only needs the contract for what to read.

options.label: AGENTS.md (parsed for declarative blocks)
options.description: Reuse the file the dev community already understands. Parse a YAML frontmatter or fenced code block (e.g. ```protostar-policy```). Risk: AGENTS.md is prose; parsing structured policy out of it is ambiguous.

options.label: New .protostar/repo-policy.json
options.description: Dedicated, schema-versioned, machine-readable. Separate from AGENTS.md (prose for humans, JSON for the kernel). Adds a new file convention to document.

options.label: Phase 2 stub — empty constant + Phase 3 fills it
options.description: Define the precedence slot but return an empty/no-op repo-instruction set. Real loading lands in Phase 3 where repo I/O is real. Phase 2 evidence shows 'repo instructions: not configured'.

questions.title: Where conflict evidence is persisted
questions.context: GOV-05 already requires admission-decision.json per gate. GOV-01 evidence could nest inside that, or be its own artifact. Phase 1 schemaVersion infrastructure is already in place.

options.label: Nested field on admission-decision.json
options.description: Add a precedenceResolution: { tiers: [...], conflicts: [...], winner: ... } field on every admission-decision.json. One artifact per gate. Inflates the schema if most gates have no conflicts.

options.label: Separate precedence-decision.json artifact
options.description: Standalone artifact emitted only when precedence is consulted. Keeps gate decisions clean. Two artifacts to read for a full picture.

options.label: Both — nested summary + separate detail
options.description: Brief summary nested in admission-decision.json (one of: 'no-conflict' | 'resolved' | 'blocked-by-tier'); full detail in precedence-decision.json when applicable.

questions.title: Per-boundary enforcement shape
questions.context: GOV-02 says envelope is enforced at every authority boundary (workspace ops, network, subprocess, budget) — 'not just stored'. Today, capability-admission.ts validates the envelope at admission time but no boundary in execution actually reads it. CONCERNS.md flags trust as 'currently a documentation field'.

options.label: Single shared guard helper
options.description: One function `assertCapabilityAllowed(envelope, op)` that every boundary calls. Easy to audit (one entrypoint). Risk: silent miss if a boundary forgets to call it — relies on test coverage.

options.label: Per-boundary middleware/wrapper functions
options.description: `withWorkspaceCapability`, `withNetworkCapability`, `withSubprocessCapability`, `withBudgetCapability` — each wraps the underlying op so calling it without the wrapper is a type error. Stronger compile-time guarantee.

options.label: Branded operation types (compile-fail without proof)
options.description: Each boundary accepts only branded inputs (e.g. `AuthorizedWorkspaceOp`); the only way to get the brand is through the capability check. Mirrors Phase 1's `ConfirmedIntent` / `AdmittedPlan` strategy. Highest ceremony, strongest guarantee.

questions.title: Subprocess + network — Phase 2 scope
questions.context: Phase 2's 'Blast Radius' in ROADMAP.md is 'Contracts only'. Phase 3 owns first real I/O. The question: does GOV-02 require runtime hooks (e.g. spawn allowlist on Node child_process) in Phase 2, or just the contract that Phase 3+ adapters must call?

options.label: Contracts + helpers only — Phase 3 wires real I/O
options.description: Phase 2 ships the guard helpers and types; the only 'real boundary' enforcement test in Phase 2 is the workspace-trust block (GOV-04). Phase 3 calls the helpers when implementing real clone/branch/write.

options.label: Contracts + a runtime sentinel that fails-closed
options.description: Ship a process-level sentinel that throws if a workspace/network/subprocess call escapes the guard helper (e.g. monkey-patch child_process.spawn in test mode to require a guard token). Catches drift. Adds dev infra.

options.label: Full runtime enforcement now (subprocess allowlist + network egress filter)
options.description: Wire actual Node hooks now. Probably out of scope vs ROADMAP 'Contracts only' framing — pulls Phase 3 work forward.

questions.title: Budget enforcement model
questions.context: Capability envelope has budget caps (tokens, time, cost). Phase 4 owns execution; Phase 5 owns the loop budget. Phase 2 needs to lock the budget-tracker contract so 4 + 5 + 6 share it.

options.label: Single shared budget tracker (one ledger per run)
options.description: One `BudgetLedger` instance per run, threaded through every adapter. Aggregates all consumption. Simple. Tight coupling: every adapter signature gains a ledger arg.

options.label: Per-boundary trackers that report to a central aggregator
options.description: Each boundary (network, subprocess, judge-panel) has its own counter; aggregator polls/sums for envelope check. Boundaries stay decoupled. More moving parts.

options.label: Phase 2 ships the contract; Phase 4 picks the impl
options.description: Define `BudgetTrackerPort` interface here, pick implementation in Phase 4 once execution is concrete. Defers lock-in. Risk: Phase 2 cannot fully test budget denial in isolation.

questions.title: Single-owner enforcement mechanism
questions.context: GOV-03: 'each artifact has a single owning package; cross-stage reads go through admission helpers'. Phase 1 already used `public-split-exports.contract.test.ts` to lock the public surface for ConfirmedIntent / AdmittedPlan. Same hammer or different?

options.label: Extend public-split-exports contract test to all artifacts
options.description: Each artifact type (admission-decision, refusal, evaluation-report, etc.) gets a contract assertion: only owning package may export the constructor / mint function. Reuses Phase 1 pattern. Single test file or per-package?

options.label: Module structure + package.json `exports` field strict
options.description: Each package's package.json declares only the public surface; private types live behind subpath conditions. TypeScript + bundler enforce it. Less custom test code; relies on toolchain.

options.label: Both — exports strictness + contract test
options.description: Belt-and-suspenders: exports field locks the runtime surface; contract test asserts the export shape and forbids constructor leak. Mirrors Phase 1 Q-03 strategy.

questions.title: Cross-stage read API shape
questions.context: Today consumers reach into `parsePlanningPileResult`, `assertAdmittedPlanHandoff`, etc. directly. GOV-03 wants 'admission helpers' to be the only path. Need a naming convention so Phase 4/5/6 know what to build against.

options.label: Per-pair helpers: `read{Artifact}From{Stage}`
options.description: e.g. `readAdmittedPlanFromPlanning(planningRunDir): Result<AdmittedPlan, AdmissionError>`. Explicit pairs. Many functions. Each helper validates schemaVersion + brand.

options.label: Generic registry: `accessArtifact(stage, type)`
options.description: Single typed registry; downstream calls a single function with discriminated args. Less code to maintain. Type-level gymnastics required for safe inference.

options.label: Stage-scoped client objects
options.description: `createPlanningStageReader()` returns a client with `.admittedPlan()`, `.refusal()`, etc. methods. Familiar OO pattern. Slightly more ceremony to construct.

questions.title: Where does the trust check live?
questions.context: Today `WorkspaceRef.trust` is declared in `packages/repo/src/index.ts:1-5` but only consumed by tests. `apps/factory-cli/src/main.ts:307` hardcodes `trust: "trusted"`. CONCERNS.md recommends a check 'in `packages/execution`'. GOV-04 specifically calls out `executionScope: "workspace"` with `trust ≠ "trusted"` as the canonical denial.

options.label: packages/policy admits/denies at envelope-validation time
options.description: Capability admission rejects before any execute grant is minted. Catches early; no code path to reach execution with bad trust. Requires the trust value at admission time (today it's set later in CLI).

options.label: packages/execution refuses at dispatch
options.description: Match CONCERNS.md recommendation. Trust is checked when the executor is asked to run a workspace op. Closer to the boundary; redundant with (a) if both exist.

options.label: Both — envelope-time admission + execution-time runtime check
options.description: Admission catches misconfigured envelopes; execution-time catches mid-run mutation/tampering. Defense in depth. The required success-criterion test (denied capability produces evidence-bearing block at the *authority boundary*) decides which is the 'authority boundary' for the test.

questions.title: CLI trust posture
questions.context: Currently `apps/factory-cli/src/main.ts:307` hardcodes `trust: "trusted"`. With GOV-04 enforced, this needs a real source. v0.0.1 lock says cosmetic-tweak loop on a Tauri sibling repo (toy / sacrificial).

options.label: Default `untrusted`; explicit `--trust trusted` flag
options.description: Safe default. Requires opt-in. Operator must type the flag every run for the dogfood loop. Discoverable.

options.label: Default from `.protostar/operator.json`; CLI flag overrides
options.description: Operator config file holds the default trust posture per repo path; CLI flag escalates per invocation. Matches the 'operator settings' precedence tier in GOV-01.

options.label: Default `untrusted`; `--trust trusted` requires a confirmed-intent flag too
options.description: Two-key launch: trust ∧ explicit confirmation. Highest friction. Right for a dark-factory posture.

questions.title: Trust failure shape
questions.context: GOV-04 success criterion: 'evidence-bearing block at the authority boundary, not the execution stage.' Block ≠ throw. Phase 1 PolicyVerdict already has `allow | needs-human | block`. Phase 2 may need an `escalate` route distinct from `needs-human`.

options.label: Block verdict only — operator must change envelope and re-run
options.description: Hard refusal; admission-decision.json captures evidence. No retry path inside the run. Simplest. Matches Phase 1 verdict shape.

options.label: Escalate verdict — pauses run; operator can confirm + resume
options.description: Adds an `escalate` outcome distinct from `block`. Useful if Phase 9 (operator surface) wants resumable approvals. Adds state machine complexity now.

options.label: Block now; reserve `escalate` shape for Phase 9
options.description: Phase 2 emits `block`; Phase 9 introduces resumable escalation. Schema-stable: `escalate` reserved as a discriminator value not yet emitted.

questions.title: Schema unification across gates
questions.context: Phase 1 already established `admission-decision.json` for the intent gate (`ADMISSION_DECISION_ARTIFACT_NAME`, `ADMISSION_DECISION_SCHEMA_VERSION` in `packages/policy/src/admission-contracts.ts`). GOV-05 expands this to capability + repo-scope + planning gates.

options.label: One shared schema with `gate` discriminator
options.description: Single `admission-decision.json` shape; field `gate: "intent" | "planning" | "capability" | "repo-scope"` selects which payload. Single schemaVersion. Easier diffing across gates. Wider schema surface area.

options.label: Per-gate schemas (intent-decision, capability-decision, ...)
options.description: Each gate gets its own filename and schemaVersion lifecycle. Independent evolution. More files. Tooling that wants 'all admission decisions for run X' must enumerate.

options.label: Per-gate filenames, shared base schema
options.description: File: `{gate}-admission-decision.json`. Schema: shared base interface + gate-specific extension. Hybrid. Matches the 'admission helper' shape from Q-09.

questions.title: Run-directory layout for gate decisions
questions.context: Phase 1 Q-08 locked `.protostar/runs/{id}/...` + `.protostar/refusals.jsonl` index. Multiple gates per run need a layout convention. The intent gate already writes to a known path inside a run dir.

options.label: Per-gate subdirectory: `runs/{id}/{gate}/admission-decision.json`
options.description: Clear layout; matches schema choice (b)/(c). Each gate is a directory. Plays well with Phase 9 inspect. Operator sees 4-6 directories per run.

options.label: Flat with discriminator filename: `runs/{id}/intent-admission.json`, `planning-admission.json`, ...
options.description: Flat directory. Matches schema choice (b)/(c). Easier to enumerate. Crowds the run dir as gates multiply.

options.label: Append-only `runs/{id}/admission-decisions.jsonl` + per-gate file
options.description: JSONL index of all gate decisions for the run + per-gate file with full payload. Mirrors `.protostar/refusals.jsonl` pattern from Phase 1 Q-08.

questions.title: Hash / signature primitive
questions.context: Phase 1 Q-13 reserved `signature: SignatureEnvelope | null`. Phase 1 Q-10 explicitly says 'Phase 2 will layer a content-hash on top for signing.' GOV-06: 'hash of intent + policy snapshot at admission time'. Note: 'admission signature' here means content-binding hash, not asymmetric signing — the threat model is detection of tampering between admission and execution, not authentication of an external signer.

options.label: SHA-256 via node:crypto (no extra deps)
options.description: Built-in. Zero deps. `crypto.createHash("sha256")`. Sufficient for tamper detection (collision-resistant). The pragmatic default.

options.label: BLAKE3 via @noble/hashes (one dep)
options.description: Faster, modern. Adds an audited dep. Marginal benefit at this throughput.

options.label: HMAC-SHA-256 with operator-provided secret
options.description: Authentication, not just integrity. Requires a secret in operator config. Closer to 'real signing'. Pulls in key management — probably more than GOV-06 calls for.

questions.title: What gets hashed (signed payload scope)
questions.context: GOV-06 says 'hash of intent + policy snapshot at admission time'. 'Policy snapshot' is fuzzy — could be the resolved capability envelope, the full policy table, or the precedence-resolution outcome.

options.label: Canonicalized intent + resolved capability envelope only
options.description: Minimal: the two things that determine what execution may do. Smallest stable surface. Misses policy-table changes (e.g. archetype policy update mid-run).

options.label: Intent + resolved envelope + policySnapshotHash
options.description: Hash references a separate policySnapshot file (the full ARCHETYPE_POLICY_TABLE state at admission time). Two-level hash chain. Detects policy drift between admission and execution.

options.label: Intent + envelope + precedence-resolution outcome (Q-04)
options.description: Includes the resolved 4-tier precedence result. Most complete: any input that produced the admission verdict is bound. Larger payload to canonicalize.

questions.title: Where verification happens
questions.context: GOV-06: 'downstream stages verify before acting'. Stages are planning admission (Phase 1, exists), execution (Phase 4), repo (Phase 3), delivery (Phase 7). Phase 2 needs to specify the verification point now.

options.label: Every downstream consumer recomputes + compares
options.description: Each stage that accepts ConfirmedIntent recomputes the hash from the canonicalized form and matches. Belt-and-suspenders. Multiple recompute sites; canonicalization must be perfectly identical everywhere.

options.label: Single central verifier helper
options.description: `verifyConfirmedIntentSignature(intent, policySnapshot)` — every stage calls it. One canonicalization site. Matches the GOV-03 'admission helper' pattern from Q-09.

options.label: Verified once at the execution-admission boundary
options.description: Only execution (the first stage that touches matter) verifies. Earlier stages trust the brand. Smaller blast radius if canonicalization changes. Risk: a non-execution stage that accepts ConfirmedIntent (e.g. Phase 6 dogpile review) might run on a tampered intent.

questions.title: SignatureEnvelope shape
questions.context: Phase 1 reserved `signature: SignatureEnvelope | null` but did not define the shape. Locking it now avoids a breaking schema change in Phase 2's first emission. JOSE/JWS is the standards-track shape; a custom shape is leaner.

options.label: Lean custom: `{ algorithm, payloadHash, signedAt }`
options.description: Three fields. `algorithm: "sha256"` for now; `payloadHash: hex string`; `signedAt: ISO-8601`. Smallest forward-compatible shape. No external standards binding.

options.label: Custom + canonicalization metadata
options.description: Adds `canonicalForm: "json-c14n@1.0"` (or similar) so future canonicalization changes are detectable. Slight forward-compat headroom. One more field to maintain.

options.label: JOSE/JWS-compatible: `{ protected, payload, signature }`
options.description: Standards-track. Future asymmetric signing is a drop-in. Heavier shape; current SHA-256-only use looks awkward inside a JWS. Probably premature for v1.
